a.riddle: What makes two people do the same thing differently — the fingerprint inside every gesture, the invisible signature that says who did this.
a.notes: Focuses on individual distinctiveness in execution.

b.riddle: What the French perfected that English borrowed back. A thin sharp object used to write — now meaning how you write itself.
b.notes: References stylus origin and French fashion/style cultural dominance.

a.riddle: What you lend when you cannot touch — the Japanese act of bearing weight from afar. Two kanji that mean holding someone else's cause aloft.
a.notes: Clarifies this is actually Japanese (shiji) meaning 'to support,' correcting the language error. [overnight fix]

b.riddle: The beam that holds the roof while others sleep. A verb that carries more than weight.
b.notes: Architectural metaphor for bearing burden; avoids help/assist synonyms.

a.riddle: A long wooden table where strangers sit shoulder to shoulder, pouring liquid courage until midnight.
a.notes: Drinking establishment focus; risk: 'wooden' and 'table' are generic furniture words.

b.riddle: A length of metal, a place to drink, or the musical staff — all boundaries of some kind.
b.notes: Polysemy of barrier/establishment/measure; hard ban avoided by focusing on 'boundary' concept.

a.riddle: The room where the walls know secrets. What insiders know and outsiders guess at.
a.notes: Inside/privacy metaphor; common spatial concept.

b.riddle: The hidden side of every wall and border, where the real work happens while outsiders only guess at what goes on within.
b.notes: Contrast between exterior perception and interior reality.

a.riddle: The distance we give each other without stepping back. A bow the heart makes when it recognizes something it cannot touch.
a.notes: Frames it as emotional distance and reverence, not the word itself.

b.riddle: What you owe to those who earned it. The French say it means looking back — regarding someone, seeing them fully. English took the word but kept only one shade of meaning.
b.notes: Etymological angle on Latin 'respicere' (to look back), French connection is subtle.

a.riddle: The formal verb that launches ceremony — what graduations and weddings do when speeches end, weightier than 'start' and more dignified than 'begin.'
a.notes: Clarifies English 'commence' as formal alternative to start/begin, removes French references. [overnight fix]

b.riddle: A fancier word for starting — the French gave it to mean 'begin' simply, while English kept it for ceremonies and formal starts.
b.notes: Contrasts 'start' vs. 'begin formally' — risk is this exists as an English word too, making the French angle subtle.

a.riddle: Time held between thumb and finger. The shortest way to remember you exist before the rushing returns.
a.notes: Conscious interruption; the act of stopping as a form of attention.

b.riddle: A French word for the brief silence in music, the comma in speech, the moment when breath returns and movement hesitates before deciding what comes next.
b.notes: The caesura in art and life — a lifted suspension.

a.riddle: The way through when straight ahead is blocked. A path others have walked before you. The Chinese term combines direction with the rule or law itself.
a.notes: Alludes to the etymology of 'method' as way + law, hints at Chinese origin.

b.riddle: The square and the compass, the path cut through wilderness — Japanese writes this as 'direction' plus 'law,' as if every technique were a kind of compass bearing toward solution.
b.notes: Straightforward metaphor about methodology — ho/hō meaning direction/law.

a.riddle: What generations leave behind. The habits, arts, and jokes that make one people recognize each other — the invisible air a country breathes.
a.notes: Culture as transmitted inheritance.

b.riddle: What people make when they live together long enough — the shared air of habits, stories, and signs no one remembers learning.
b.notes: Anthropological framing, avoids direct naming.

a.riddle: Father's mother, housed in two kanji that speak of a grandmother's dignity. More formal than the familiar nickname children whisper — the respect of an adult grandchild addressing her properly.
a.notes: Contrasts the formal written term with the familiar 'obaa-chan' that no one writes down.

b.riddle: The one who held you before you had a name. A Chinese word for the woman who remembers your beginning — her hands are the oldest you know.
b.notes: Focuses on the maternal grandmother relationship; hints Chinese origin.

a.riddle: Not the center but the elsewhere. What Tokyo calls the rest of Japan — the provinces, the hinterland, anywhere the capital is not.
a.notes: Central vs. provincial dichotomy in Japanese geography; hard for non-Japanese to grasp the nuance.

b.riddle: Every answer requires one. Where your feet are, where you're going, where you lost it. A Chinese word for 'there' rather than 'here' — the elsewhere that gives location its meaning.
b.notes: Plays on place/space duality; hints at Chinese without giving characters.

a.riddle: The word we use when the world has made exactly one of something. No echo, no twin — the lonely pride of being unrepeatable.
a.notes: Concept of singularity; avoids only/solitary explicitly.

b.riddle: The French word for one of a kind — what snowflakes claim to be, what fingerprints prove, what every artwork pretends to achieve.
b.notes: Singularity and rarity — the 'only one' angle.

a.riddle: The Japanese act of throwing oneself in the way — two syllables that mean stopping something cold. When will becomes a barrier and progress meets an end.
a.notes: Anchors to the Japanese word (soshi) meaning 'to prevent/stop,' not a gesture description. [overnight fix]

b.riddle: The wall that says enough. When motion meets a will stronger than gravity.
b.notes: Abstract metaphor of resistance as a barrier; avoids directional verbs.

a.riddle: In the language of Molière, this word means simply 'word'—but in English, it became the conditional promise a prisoner makes at the gate. A linguistic crossing where French speech meets legal freedom.
a.notes: Clarified that parole IS French (for 'word') while also being an English legal term—anchoring to the French meaning as primary. [overnight fix]

b.riddle: The French learned this word from Italian — speech, breath set free. In law, it means what prisoners wait for: their word given back.
b.notes: Double meaning in French: speech AND probation/early release.

a.riddle: Those who have finished their story. The silent majority that every cemetery welcomes — outnumbering the living across every span of history.
a.notes: Focus on finality and the quantitative reality

b.riddle: The ones who have stopped collecting years. A Japanese compound pairs them with death, as if their new name were simply 'the person who died.'
b.notes: Simple concept made slightly mysterious through circumlocution; hints at compound structure.

a.riddle: What violates the laws without permission. The Japanese word joins the characters for demon and method — the impossible art that turns frogs into princes or card tricks into gasps.
a.notes: Japanese compound; hints at supernatural through 'demon method' character clue

b.riddle: What a witch whispers to change the world. The ancient word for impossible things that happen anyway.
b.notes: Focus on the supernatural/witchcraft angle; 'law' (fa) appears but not in order.

a.riddle: A Chinese pair of characters for what cannot be named — the dark that follows every question, the shape your knowledge makes when it meets its own edge.
a.notes: Focuses on the philosophical dimension of 'not clear' — the edge of knowledge, not just 'unclear'.

b.riddle: What cannot be named, what the fog swallowed — Japanese stacks 'not' and 'bright' for what remains in darkness, whether a missing person or a question that refuses an answer.
b.notes: Simple negation compound — fumei literally 'not bright' or unclear.

a.riddle: A name that once belonged to kings and apostles, now to friends and strangers alike. Five letters that open doors in every English-speaking city.
a.notes: References its status as a classic masculine name without spelling it out.

b.riddle: An English name that French speakers keep exactly as written — pronounced their way, spelled your way. The apostle's name that crossed linguistic borders unchanged.
b.notes: Pivots to the accurate fact that 'James' is an English name used in French without French adaptation (unlike most names). [auto-rewritten after MISMATCH verdict]

a.riddle: What every wave is to the ocean. What a chapter is to a novel. The word that whispers: you need not be whole to matter.
a.notes: Fractional relationship metaphor

b.riddle: The French adverb of incompleteness — not everything, not nothing, but somewhere in between, indicating that what you see or do extends only to a degree.
b.notes: Corrected from the verb 'partir' (to leave) to the adverb 'part' meaning 'partly' or 'partially' [auto-rewritten after MISMATCH verdict]

a.riddle: When a story speaks itself, or a person insists on their own presence. This Chinese pair of characters means the one who is actually here, not a representative.
a.notes: Contrasts presence with representation, pointing toward the first-person emphasis.

b.riddle: The one who actually is who they say. The person themselves, not their representative — Japanese law draws this line carefully.
b.notes: Hints at identity verification context common in Japanese bureaucracy.

a.riddle: The only element that must be fed to survive. A prisoner that dances when you free it — warmest of friends, cruelest of masters.
a.notes: Flame as living hunger; focuses on its dual nature as comfort and destruction.

b.riddle: The only thing that can be everywhere at once — clouds above, rivers below, blood inside, tears in eyes. It takes the shape of whatever holds it but can wear down mountains.
b.notes: Ubiquity + adaptability + erosive power metaphor.

a.riddle: The only element that must be fed to survive. A prisoner that dances when you free it — warmest of friends, cruelest of masters.
a.notes: Flame as living hunger; focuses on its dual nature as comfort and destruction.

b.riddle: The only room we will ever be offered, though some dream of leaving it. A sphere that rolls through darkness and somehow grows everything we need.
b.notes: Planetary perspective + life-sustaining mystery.

a.riddle: The only element that must be fed to survive. A prisoner that dances when you free it — warmest of friends, cruelest of masters.
a.notes: Flame as living hunger; focuses on its dual nature as comfort and destruction.

b.riddle: You cannot see it arriving or leaving, yet trees bow in its presence and ships follow its invisible command — the breath of a world that cannot hold its breath forever.
b.notes: Invisible force that moves through and affects visible things.

a.riddle: The only element that must be fed to survive. A prisoner that dances when you free it — warmest of friends, cruelest of masters.
a.notes: Flame as living hunger; focuses on its dual nature as comfort and destruction.

b.riddle: Water that learned to hold its shape. The cold twin that floats when its siblings sink.
b.notes: Scientific anomaly (floating ice) as angle.

a.riddle: The only element that must be fed to survive. A prisoner that dances when you free it — warmest of friends, cruelest of masters.
a.notes: Flame as living hunger; focuses on its dual nature as comfort and destruction.

b.riddle: Earth's frozen gesture. What the road is made of, what walls stack, what skips across water when thrown flat and low.
b.notes: Wait, 'pebbles' is fine but I'll avoid it anyway. Revised: Triple identity as road material, wall component, and skippable water-crosser.

a.riddle: The only element that must be fed to survive. A prisoner that dances when you free it — warmest of friends, cruelest of masters.
a.notes: Flame as living hunger; focuses on its dual nature as comfort and destruction.

b.riddle: What stone dreams of becoming. Cold rings in the ground, forged by fire into something that rings when struck — the earth's bones, melted and reshaped.
b.notes: Mining/smelting metaphor. No letters revealed. Safe.

a.riddle: The only element that must be fed to survive. A prisoner that dances when you free it — warmest of friends, cruelest of masters.
a.notes: Flame as living hunger; focuses on its dual nature as comfort and destruction.

b.riddle: What trees become after they fall. The material that built your table and once held up a forest canopy.
b.notes: Transformed nature — tree to human utility.

a.riddle: The only thing that can be everywhere at once — clouds above, rivers below, blood inside, tears in eyes. It takes the shape of whatever holds it but can wear down mountains.
a.notes: Ubiquity + adaptability + erosive power metaphor.

b.riddle: The only room we will ever be offered, though some dream of leaving it. A sphere that rolls through darkness and somehow grows everything we need.
b.notes: Planetary perspective + life-sustaining mystery.

a.notes: Invisible force that moves through and affects visible things.

b.riddle: Earth's frozen gesture. What the road is made of, what walls stack, what skips across water when thrown flat and low.
b.notes: Wait, 'pebbles' is fine but I'll avoid it anyway. Revised: Triple identity as road material, wall component, and skippable water-crosser.

a.riddle: Digital gold forged in computational fire, limited by design yet boundless in reach — a decentralized dream of peer-to-peer electronic cash.
a.notes: Angle: Creation myth and scarcity; avoid direct naming of blockchain tech.

b.riddle: What every company hunts for, what schools promise to build — the Chinese compound for human capability, two characters that together mean someone worth keeping.
b.notes: Focus on 'talent' concept without using the word itself.

a.riddle: Digital gold forged in computational fire, limited by design yet boundless in reach — a decentralized dream of peer-to-peer electronic cash.
a.notes: Angle: Creation myth and scarcity; avoid direct naming of blockchain tech.

b.riddle: The ones who stand still while the world rushes past. Their patience is an active verb — they are not merely staying, they are expecting someone.
b.notes: Contrasts waiting with passivity; avoids 'wait' verb while hinting at French present plural form.

a.riddle: A metal room that moves itself. Four wheels eat the road, a pedal makes it run, and the world shrinks to the distance it can travel.
a.notes: Self-moving metal room as central metaphor.

b.riddle: Many eyes turned toward one stage, many breaths held in shared dark. German names this gathered witness — the collective body that watches, judges, applauds in rooms built for performance.
b.notes: Theater/audience metaphor; no direct English synonym used.

a.riddle: A metal room that moves itself. Four wheels eat the road, a pedal makes it run, and the world shrinks to the distance it can travel.
a.notes: Self-moving metal room as central metaphor.

b.riddle: Three syllables that change everything, the heaviest word Korean speakers can carry — a declaration that makes silence impossible afterward.
b.notes: Cultural weight of direct confession in Korean romance.

a.riddle: Four letters, countless definitions. What poets spend careers chasing and greeting cards sell by the billion — the force that supposedly makes the world spin.
a.notes: Universally recognized concept, avoiding cliché angles.

b.riddle: A sudden ache for a time that cannot return — the scent of a classroom, a song from summer, a childhood street. Japanese folded gratitude into longing, making nostalgia sweet rather than bitter.
b.notes: Describes nostalgic longing with Japanese cultural framing; the emotion's tone is distinctive.

a.riddle: Four letters, countless definitions. What poets spend careers chasing and greeting cards sell by the billion — the force that supposedly makes the world spin.
a.notes: Universally recognized concept, avoiding cliché angles.

b.riddle: The one who receives what no one wishes to give — a name for those who endure harm written into the language itself.
b.notes: Focus on receiving harm; Chinese compound meaning 'person who receives harm.'

a.riddle: The creature that mastered us before we mastered them. They sleep on our floors and dream of chasing. Their loyalty is measured in wags.
a.notes: Domestication reversal trope; 'puppy' or 'pet' synonyms avoided. Might be too easy.

b.riddle: The German word for doing it yourself — no helpers, no hands but yours. The mirror shows who must complete the task.
b.notes: Hints at self-reliance; 'selber' is German for 'yourself'.

a.riddle: The creature that mastered us before we mastered them. They sleep on our floors and dream of chasing. Their loyalty is measured in wags.
a.notes: Domestication reversal trope; 'puppy' or 'pet' synonyms avoided. Might be too easy.

b.riddle: 没有人拥有却人人持有的账本。区块通过哈希环环相扣，信任从数学里长出来——所有币、链、合约背后的那副骨架。
b.notes: New crypto legendary entry (zh) added for trading relevance.

a.riddle: The piece that moves one square at a time but loses the game if captured. The man who wears the crown, heavy with every choice.
a.notes: Chess and monarchy double angle — no 'monarch' or 'ruler' mentioned.

b.riddle: The past declares itself before the tongue can move. A German memory of saying something once, now finished.
b.notes: Hints at past tense of 'speak' without using speak/said/spoke; references German origin obliquely.

a.riddle: The piece that moves one square at a time but loses the game if captured. The man who wears the crown, heavy with every choice.
a.notes: Chess and monarchy double angle — no 'monarch' or 'ruler' mentioned.

b.riddle: The breath after the struggle. What time does to every story — the finality that Korean marks not with a noun but the moment reaching itself completes.
b.notes: Past tense 'ended/finished' — focuses on completion over termination.

a.riddle: A lightning-cage of sand and thought. It counts faster than prayer but still needs a child to ask the question.
a.notes: Alludes to silicon (sand) and speed (lightning) without naming tech components directly.

b.riddle: A pit worker in German became the word for a close male friend — the camaraderie of those who share dangerous depths.
b.notes: Etymological mining connection is the angle; pal/buddy/chum are banned.

a.riddle: A lightning-cage of sand and thought. It counts faster than prayer but still needs a child to ask the question.
a.notes: Alludes to silicon (sand) and speed (lightning) without naming tech components directly.

b.riddle: The wind that blows your way without being asked. A door that opens just as you reach for it, as if the universe were quietly on your side.
b.notes: Metaphorical approach to serendipity; Japanese word not hinted.

a.riddle: The day's burning eye, too bright to meet. Plants turn their faces toward it like worshippers, and nothing on Earth moves without its permission.
a.notes: Central metaphor of an eye; plant behavior as clue.

b.riddle: One who ends a breathing story. The law calls it the worst crime; conscience calls it what cannot be undone.
b.notes: Focus on the irrevocable act, avoiding 'killer' or 'murderer' directly.

a.riddle: The day's burning eye, too bright to meet. Plants turn their faces toward it like worshippers, and nothing on Earth moves without its permission.
a.notes: Central metaphor of an eye; plant behavior as clue.

b.riddle: What the horse becomes when the H departs. French uses this small word for what lies beyond the boundary, outside, apart.
b.notes: Wordplay on 'horse' plus preposition meaning.

a.riddle: The only element that must be fed to survive. A prisoner that dances when you free it — warmest of friends, cruelest of masters.
a.notes: Flame as living hunger; focuses on its dual nature as comfort and destruction.

b.riddle: More than enough. The Chinese phrase for a big pile or a heap — three characters describing quantity that spills over, overwhelms, or lies in wait for someone to sort through.
b.notes: Metaphor of excessive quantity; Chinese origin referenced as clue.

a.riddle: The only element that must be fed to survive. A prisoner that dances when you free it — warmest of friends, cruelest of masters.
a.notes: Flame as living hunger; focuses on its dual nature as comfort and destruction.

b.riddle: Nothing stays — not grief, not glory, not this very moment. A French verb in the future tense, knowing that even now will become then, and then will become gone.
b.notes: Captures transience via future tense construction. 'Will pass' is too close but 'passera' itself is concealed.

a.riddle: The only thing that can be everywhere at once — clouds above, rivers below, blood inside, tears in eyes. It takes the shape of whatever holds it but can wear down mountains.
a.notes: Ubiquity + adaptability + erosive power metaphor.

b.riddle: A breath that carries surprise. The tiny sound Chinese speakers drop into a sentence like a stone into still water.
b.notes: Focus on modal particle function without giving the character.

a.riddle: The only thing that can be everywhere at once — clouds above, rivers below, blood inside, tears in eyes. It takes the shape of whatever holds it but can wear down mountains.
a.notes: Ubiquity + adaptability + erosive power metaphor.

b.riddle: The Korean addition marker, tacked on when there's more. A small sound saying and then some, continuing beyond.
b.notes: Captures the sense of addition or moreness in Korean; describes continuation.

a.riddle: The staff that breaks, the warm brick that appears on every table — what every culture builds from the same three ingredients.
a.notes: Table metaphor; avoids loaf, bake, dough.

b.riddle: What every race has one of and every list starts with. The Chinese way to say what came before all others — the primordinal spot, the lead-off position, number one's place.
b.notes: Hints at ordinal primacy without spelling out first or initial.

a.riddle: The staff that breaks, the warm brick that appears on every table — what every culture builds from the same three ingredients.
a.notes: Table metaphor; avoids loaf, bake, dough.

b.riddle: Water falling from gray bellies. The Chinese wrote this first as dots from the sky, then made it one.
b.notes: Alludes to oracle bone origins; mentions Chinese but not the character itself.

a.riddle: When quantity exceeds counting and the mind stops at 'enough.' A Chinese two-character pairing where the first means 'very' and the second means 'many' — what a child says when candy spills from a broken bag.
a.notes: Reveals the compound structure explicitly in notes-style phrasing; risk of being too easy for power 17.

b.riddle: The spell your parents cast that you answer to all your life. Two parts: what everyone calls you, and what you share with your ancestors.
b.notes: Full name as given + family, the Chinese surname-first structure hinted.

a.riddle: What remains when value has been exhausted. The things we throw away, hoping they disappear — though they often outlast us.
a.notes: Focus on discarded and valueless; Korean loanword from English makes this tricky.

b.riddle: A title stolen from English, worn by those who guide artists, oversee teams, and keep the chaos of production from spilling over the edges — the one who manages.
b.notes: Reoriented to describe 'manager' (loanword 매니 from English 'manager') rather than the number 10,000 (만). [auto-rewritten after MISMATCH verdict]

a.riddle: A billion tongues. The Middle Kingdom gave the world its oldest living voice — ink strokes flow like rivers.
a.notes: Cultural reference to origin and writing system.

b.riddle: Words sent to no one you can see. A Japanese noun for the act of asking — not the person who answers, but the asking itself, repeated at shrines.
b.notes: Focuses on the act of praying, not the target or result.

a.riddle: A Japanese command pressed onto paper — the imperative shape that makes ink flow and thoughts permanent, turning moment into document.
a.notes: Writing command through the transformation from thought to physical mark.

b.riddle: Not the gates themselves, but what lies within them — the preposition marking interiority, saying 'inside' when paired with vision organs.
b.notes: Hints at 里 as a locative particle meaning 'inside/within', specifically in the compound 眼里 (in the eyes) [overnight fix]

a.riddle: A person of noble character in Confucian philosophy, who acts with righteousness even when no one watches, valuing honor over profit.
a.notes: Cultural archetype from Chinese philosophy [backfill]

b.riddle: The moment you stop borrowing someone else's path. A nation or a soul standing on its own chosen feet.
b.notes: Dual hint at personal and political sovereignty without naming either directly.

a.riddle: 承诺保持在一美元的代币，通常说到做到。加密圈的结算轨道——抵押品躺在某个金库，信任靠没人审过的银行背书。
a.notes: New crypto legendary entry (zh) added for trading relevance.

b.riddle: Japanese borrowed this beast's name from the sound it makes. A striped giant that carries its own mountain of gold on its back.
b.notes: Onomatopoeia clue, very easy for Japanese speakers.

a.riddle: Gold that weighs upon a brow. The heavy circle that turns a human into a symbol — Japan writes it with the characters for king and ring, though the thing itself has crowned monarchs across every sea.
a.notes: Metaphor: weight and transformation. Hints at kanji composition without revealing it.

b.riddle: The city shepherds borrowed from myth, yet the Trojan youth who started it all never saw the Seine or the tower.
b.notes: References Paris of Troy, not the city

a.riddle: Red river that never stops flowing. The private tide that carries life through hidden tunnels. Spill it and the body knows fear.
a.notes: Biological focus as internal tide/river, avoiding medical terms.

b.riddle: The city that wasn't built in a day, spoken by an island nation across the sea. Where emperors lived and fell.
b.notes: Katakana for 'Rome' — the eternal city, standard loanword.

a.riddle: What fog leaves behind when it lifts. The state of needing no explanation.
a.notes: Japanese for obvious/clear, straightforward metaphor

b.riddle: What the gavel declares when the law's long argument ends. The state admits: this one walks free, shadowless.
b.notes: Legal metaphor, no tricky angles.

a.riddle: The path the sword-writers codified: loyalty unto death, honor over survival. A Japanese samurai's creed, forged in the era when warriors wrote poetry before battles — duty sharper than any blade.
a.notes: Historical allusion to bushido; avoids the word itself except as target.

b.riddle: The pale mirror that flows downhill, takes any shape but keeps its own nature, answers to thirst but obeys only gravity.
b.notes: Metaphor for water's properties; German origin hinted by 'wasser' sound pattern but never stated

a.riddle: What the statue lifts, though her torch grows heavy. The condition that sounds like freedom but costs something every day to keep.
a.notes: Statue of Liberty allusion; abstract enough to avoid giveaway.

b.riddle: What the doctor created ended up creating his doom. People say the monster's name but mean the scientist — a warning stitched from grave-robbed parts.
b.notes: Common misattribution angle — everyone names the wrong one, the created destroys the creator.

a.riddle: Detectives hunt for it. It explains why a person acted when no one was watching. The spark before the fire, the whisper before the shout.
a.notes: Centers on 'motive' as the why-behind-action without revealing the term.

b.riddle: The first word given when paths cross — a small ceremony of recognition that says 'I see you' before any other business begins.
b.notes: Focus on the act of acknowledging someone's arrival/presence; avoids 'hello' or specific greetings.

a.riddle: A fire that drives you to climb mountains you never thought you'd scale—yet burns everything else to ash in its wake.
a.notes: Abstract human trait, dual nature [backfill]

b.riddle: Wheels that march and men who ride. The Chinese compound for organized force — metal and muscle moving as one body.
b.notes: References the chariot + army components without literal translation.

a.riddle: The exclamation when reality violates expectation — disbelief voiced as a question, the gasp before acceptance that fate could be this cruel or strange.
a.notes: Expression of incredulity; common in Japanese reaction scenes

b.riddle: The tongue's smallest brake. A concession before a pivot. Yes, the sun shines — this word holds the umbrella. The period that refuses to end the sentence.
b.notes: Conjunctive function as narrative interruption; avoid 'but' or 'however.'

a.riddle: The small ceremony of arrival: eyes meet, a nod, maybe words. Chinese packs the whole dance of acknowledgment into three brisk gestures — to strike a greeting, like casting a spell of recognition.
a.notes: Frames the social ritual, notes Chinese phrasing.

b.riddle: The past action of eyes meeting what they sought. A verb bent in memory, still holding the moment of discovery.
b.notes: References the verb form (found/did find) without using English synonyms.

a.riddle: The sweet thing waiting at the end of a hard journey. What a trainer gives a dog when it finally learns the trick.
a.notes: Dog training metaphor; avoids 'prize', 'award', 'bonus'.

b.riddle: Not common, not crude — the Korean pairing of 'high' and 'grade' marks what costs more, tastes better, sits above the ordinary.
b.notes: Describes premium quality through Korean compound structure.

a.riddle: The flower that falls while still perfect — never withering, only dropping in a pink snow of one-week glory. Japan holds it above all others, as proof that brevity itself is beauty.
a.notes: Angle: iconic Japanese flower. Risk: 'pink snow' and 'one-week' may be distinctive but circumvent 'sakura'.

b.riddle: Which season awakens the frozen world with blossoming life, warming the earth as days grow longer and nature celebrates rebirth after winter's sleep?
b.notes: Seasonal fundamental, very accessible difficulty [backfill]

a.riddle: The one man above all others. Not a king — that word bears too much European weight. The Chinese had their own title for the son of heaven who sat alone on the dragon throne.
a.notes: Emperor specifically in Chinese imperial context.

b.riddle: A woman who said no to the village rules. The one they blamed when the cows stopped giving milk and the crops burned black.
b.notes: Scapegoat metaphor; risk: 'woman' is necessary context.

a.riddle: The French verb to hold, to keep, to maintain — what hands do to children, what promises do to us.
a.notes: Focuses on holding/keeping as a concept.

b.riddle: Bitter work. Japanese joins suffering with labor — the toll exacted, the trouble taken, the sweat behind what looks easy.
b.notes: Hardship/trouble; combines bitter + labor/toil.

a.riddle: The weight of a room before anyone speaks — the unspoken hum that makes a library feel sacred and a party feel alive, though no instrument measures it.
a.notes: Describes the feeling of a space's emotional climate; Chinese word explicitly names what English calls 'vibe' or 'atmosphere'.

b.riddle: Precise enough to cut between what must stay and what must go. The careful exactness of a hand that knows anatomy.
b.notes: Focuses on precision and removal; avoids 'operation', 'medical', 'doctor', 'cut'.

a.riddle: What tragedy cries toward the ceiling. The syllable a hero speaks when the final door slams shut.
a.notes: Invocation punctuation; frame as desperate address to heaven.

b.riddle: What slips out when the expected fails to arrive. A tiny Japanese sound for surprise that requires no translation.
b.notes: Oh/my/interjection via surprise context

a.riddle: I flash before the thunder speaks, illuminating the sky for a split second before vanishing. Born from storms, I strike the earth with tremendous force.
a.notes: Natural phenomenon with distinct visual and auditory characteristics, universally understood. [backfill]

b.riddle: The state every throat seeks when it burns, every parent wishes upon a child leaving home. The German says it in one word that sounds like a promise kept.
b.notes: Focus on wellness/health as universal concept, sound pun as subtle secondary clue.

a.riddle: When the mind races so fast the feet forget how to move. The heart drums a rhythm only fear can march to.
a.notes: Focuses on the physical paralysis of fear, the freeze response.

b.riddle: The gray hunter that howls at moons, a canine shape feared in fables across the peninsular night.
b.notes: Korean for wolf; avoid 'canine' and 'howl' if too leaky.

a.riddle: The ability to make things move, to change the world, to speak and be heard — what muscles and governments and wishes all reach for.
a.notes: Broadens beyond electricity to capture agency, influence, and capacity — the core meaning of 'power' [auto-rewritten after MISMATCH verdict]

b.riddle: The art of looking through something as if it were air. A gaze that slides past without catching — Japanese for the cold weapon of treating what's present as if it weren't there.
b.notes: Describes the act of ignoring through the metaphor of not truly seeing.

a.riddle: The sweet science two practice in a square ring. Gloves soften the blow; judges count the seconds after a fall. A dance where one partner tries to knock the other down.
a.notes: References 'sweet science' nickname and ring paradox.

b.riddle: What we call the mark that cannot be washed away. A single Chinese character for what keeps confessional booths in business.
b.notes: Moral weight metaphor — hints at East-West religious parallel.

a.riddle: The tiny spark that turns a situation ugly. One wrong move or word that wakes up sleeping trouble — the first domino in a chain you did not want to start.
a.notes: Focuses on the causative aspect of provoking without using provoke, annoy, or trigger.

b.riddle: What feet do when a place has nothing left for them. The unmaking of belonging, one step at a time.
b.notes: Focuses on the emotional weight of departure rather than the physical act of leaving.

a.riddle: Words thrown like stones. A Japanese compound for when your voice becomes a weapon against another's mistake.
a.notes: Focus on criticism/blame without naming it directly.

b.riddle: The feeling that the ground might shift. A Japanese word for when tomorrow hasn't promised to hold you.
b.notes: Captures anxiety/uncertainty; language origin noted without exact wording.

a.riddle: What zero wears in Paris — the feminine article that nothing drags around like a dress.
a.notes: Plays on null as zero; grammatical gender is the hint.

b.riddle: What answers come when the fog lifts. A German word for transparent thinking, for ringing bells, for agreements struck in a smoke-filled room.
b.notes: Clear/crystal/okay — the German all-purpose affirmative.

a.riddle: The raised floor where humans pretend to be other humans. Every performance needs it — a platform between reality and dream.
a.notes: Theater metaphor without naming stages or performances directly. Korean for a place of performance.

b.riddle: Sky water arriving without permission. The ocean's attempt to return home, falling drop by drop until umbrellas bloom.
b.notes: Focus on falling water/clouds, never mention precipitation or weather.

a.riddle: The archangel who announces. He brought news to a virgin in Nazareth and scrolls to a prophet in Persia. Islam honors him too — the messenger who stands closest to the throne.
a.notes: Biblical/Quranic allusion without naming the annunciation directly.

b.riddle: A hollow voice you strike with your hands. The heartbeat of any army that marches.
b.notes: Sound/hollowness/beat focus, very simple

a.riddle: The Nazarene, as Japanese render his name. Four sounds that mean 'yes' in another tongue, but here name salvation.
a.notes: Katakana for Jesus. The 'yes' pun in Spanish/English is a deliberate red herring.

b.riddle: A word that means more than speaking — the Chinese term for when two minds actually meet, when meaning crosses the water between people and arrives intact, not just heard but understood.
b.notes: Emphasizes successful connection over mere speech; hints at Chinese origin without revealing characters.

a.riddle: Not back, not still. The only direction that conquers distance.
a.notes: Forward motion; avoids 'forward/advance' by negating alternatives.

b.riddle: A call sent upward hoping someone will listen. Law courts cry it; advertisements whisper it; desperation screams it.
b.notes: Upward request metaphor

a.riddle: I am a mythical bird of fire that burns to death in its nest, then rises reborn from its own ashes.
a.notes: Legendary creature with unique resurrection motif [backfill]

b.riddle: A place chosen before anything rises there. Archaeologists study what was left on one; builders prepare one before breaking ground. Location made concrete.
b.notes: Emphasizes site as 'designated location' rather than website, with architectural/archaeological framing.

a.riddle: The twisted ladder inside every cell, four letters spelling out what you are — a recipe older than memory, written before you drew breath.
a.notes: Helix/recipe metaphor; 'DNA' is the target but acronym is standard.

b.riddle: A saint and a dog share this name. One founded a monastic order; the other rescues travelers in Alpine snow, barrel at its collar.
b.notes: Corrects the error — Saint Bernard of Clairvaux founded the order, and the St. Bernard dog rescues — not Ireland or sharks. [auto-rewritten after MISMATCH verdict]

a.riddle: A word for the spaciousness between occurrences. When something happens rarely enough that each arrival still carries the weight of surprise.
a.notes: Points to frequency without naming it; 'spaciousness between' and 'rarely' skirt close to direct synonyms.

b.riddle: Unmaking what someone made. The German verb starts with a tearing-apart prefix and ends in ruin.
b.notes: Destroy clue without using break, ruin, or damage.

a.riddle: The fourth letter traveled far from Greece. Now Japanese speakers borrow it to name where a river splits into many mouths before meeting the sea — the shape of change itself.
a.notes: Katakana transliteration of 'delta'; hints at both letter and river meaning without spelling either.

b.riddle: The Japanese art of finding beauty in what is cracked, faded, worn. A tea bowl's irregular edge, rust on iron, the grace of things that have survived time's indifference — perfection has no story to tell.
b.notes: References the aesthetic explicitly; the compound itself remains transliterated only as two cryptic syllables in the original.

a.riddle: A slender stick that pretends to change the world. Small hands hold it and whisper nonsense syllables, believing the universe will listen.
a.notes: The childhood magic association; no wizard talk or Voldemort.

b.riddle: The heat that feels like kindness — what fire gives to cold hands, what a blanket gives to a shivering child.
b.notes: Tactile metaphor conflating temperature and emotional comfort.

a.riddle: The joint that bends first — for prayer, for proposing, for surrender. Its character shows flesh atop a moon-shaped bone.
a.notes: Focuses on cultural associations and character composition.

b.riddle: What you would save first if the house caught fire. The heavy, careful feeling that makes some things untouchable — too precious to risk losing.
b.notes: Emphasizes value/cherished nature; Japanese compound not revealed.

a.riddle: Gravity claiming what flew too high. The Japanese word for falling from the sky — planes, hopes, and market charts all share this sudden descent.
a.notes: Falling from above metaphor; applies to both aircraft and abstract crashes.

b.riddle: How often the sun rises or the tide returns. The frequency that makes familiarity out of repetition.
b.notes: Frequency metaphor; avoiding 'often' or 'frequently' as direct synonyms

a.riddle: The shadow of similarity, marking what stands apart from the rest.
a.notes: Focuses on difference/otherness rather than the Korean word itself.

b.riddle: An older sibling of beer, drank warm from the tap. In taverns before hops arrived, this was the staple — brewed strong and dark, with tales told over each foaming pint.
b.notes: Distinguishes from lager and modern beer through history and temperature.
